criteria: The agent has read Section 3.2 completely to understand the two-stage overview of SAFM (decision stage and tuning stage).
The agent has read Section 3.3 completely to understand the decision stage, including the warmup procedure and architecture search procedure.
The agent has read Equation (1) to understand how the hidden state h_n^l is computed as a weighted average of all candidate adapter outputs.
The agent has read Equation (2) to understand how the weight vector λ^l is initialized using the sparse factor α and reuse factor β via softmax.
The agent has read Section 3.4 completely to understand the tuning stage, including the pseudo-replay mechanism and the layer-wise loss.
The agent has read Equations (3) and (4) to understand how the layer-wise loss is computed as cosine similarity between adjacent adapter hidden states and summed across all layers.
The agent has read Section 4.1 to understand the two experimental scenarios (similar and dissimilar) and the datasets used in each.

The agent has read Section 4.3 to check all implementation details: GPT-2 backbone, AdamW optimizer, learning rate 1.75e-4, batch size 8, decision stage epochs 6 (3 warmup + 3 AS), tuning stage epochs 12, α=0.11, β=0.08, no-AS layers, pseudo-replay rate 0.2, and layer-wise loss weights.
The agent has read Table 1 to check the main results comparing SAFM with all baselines on Score, BWT, and learnable parameters across both scenarios.
The agent has read Table 2 to check the ablation study comparing ACM, SAFM (w/o layer-wise), and SAFM across all eight task orders.
The agent has read Table 3 to check the ablation study on different pseudo-sample ratios (0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 0.8) for SAFM versus ACM at ratio 0.2.
The agent has read Table 4 to check the ablation study on the effect of fixing no architecture search (no-AS) in specific adapter layers.
The agent has read Table 6 to check the scale-up experiments with different backbone model sizes (GPT-2 124M, 355M, 774M, and Llama3-8B).
The agent has read Appendix C (Table 8) to understand all eight task orders used in the similar (Orders 1-4) and dissimilar (Orders 5-8) scenarios.
The plan includes implementing SAFM as a two-stage framework: a decision stage (warmup + architecture search) followed by a tuning stage (pseudo-replay + layer-wise loss).
The plan includes three types of adapter candidates per layer: the empty adapter A_e, k unique prior adapters relabeled as μ^l = {μ_1^l, ..., μ_k^l}, and the newly initialized adapter A_n^l.
The plan includes the warmup procedure: initializing a task-specific adapter set A_n = {A_n^1, ..., A_n^L} across all L transformer layers for each new task T_n.
The plan includes the architecture search procedure: computing the weight vector λ^l using Equation (2) and selecting the candidate adapter with the highest weight as the final A_n^l for each layer.

The plan includes the pseudo-replay mechanism: generating pseudo-samples P_i from each previously learned task T_i whose adapter is reused by the current task, and combining them with current task data D_n during the tuning stage.
The plan includes the layer-wise loss mechanism using cosine similarity between hidden states of adjacent adapter layers within the same task, as defined in Equations (3) and (4).
The plan includes loading the similar scenario datasets: E2ENLG, RNNLG (restaurant, hotel, TV, laptop), SGD, TM19, TM20, spanning 14 domains with four task orders (Orders 1-4).
The plan includes loading the dissimilar scenario datasets: E2ENLG, RNNLG, WikiSQL, CNN/DailyMail, SGD, TM19, TM20, spanning 14 domains with four task orders (Orders 5-8).
The plan includes all 10 baseline methods: Finetune, EWC, LAMOL, InsCL, AdapterCL, Adapter+LAMOL, ACM, O-LoRA, SAPT, and Multi.
The plan includes implementing evaluation metrics per task type: ACC for INTENT, JGA for DST, BLEU for NLG and Summarization, and EM for SQL Query Generation.
The plan includes computing the two overall evaluation metrics: Average Score as (1/t) Σ R_{N,i} and BWT as (1/(t-1)) Σ (R_{N,i} - R_{i,i}).
The plan specifies using GPT-2 as the backbone with AdamW optimizer (lr=1.75e-4), batch size 8, sparse factor α=0.11, reuse factor β=0.08, and pseudo-replay rate 0.2.
The plan includes the ablation study variant SAFM (w/o layer-wise) which uses architecture search but omits the layer-wise loss.
The plan includes ablation experiments on pseudo-sample ratios (0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 0.8) tested on Order 1 (similar) and Order 5 (dissimilar).
The plan includes scale-up experiments comparing SAFM and ACM on GPT-2 (124M, 355M, 774M) and Llama3-8B, measuring scores after task 8 and task 10 in Order 1.
The adapter module has been implemented as a residual bottleneck adapter inserted after each transformer layer, following Houlsby et al. (2019).
The empty adapter A_e has been implemented as a pass-through module that produces zero contribution (no trainable parameters and no output transformation).
The warmup procedure has been implemented to initialize a new adapter set A_n = {A_n^1, ..., A_n^L} across all L transformer layers for each incoming task T_n before the architecture search.
The prior adapter relabeling step has been implemented: unique adapters from previous tasks at layer l are collected and relabeled as μ^l = {μ_1^l, ..., μ_k^l} where k ≤ t-1.

The candidate adapter set c^l has been implemented to include the empty adapter A_e, the k relabeled prior adapters {μ_1^l, ..., μ_k^l}, and the newly initialized adapter A_n^l, forming k+2 candidates.
The hidden state computation has been implemented as Equation (1): h_n^l = λ_e^l × A_e(f_n^l(h_n^{l-1})) + Σ_{i=1}^{k} λ_i^l × μ_i^l(f_n^l(h_n^{l-1})) + λ_n^l × A_n^l(f_n^l(h_n^{l-1})).
The weight vector initialization has been implemented as Equation (2): λ_e^l = e^α/φ, λ_j^l = e^β/φ for j∈[1,k], and λ_n^l = e^{-β}/φ, where φ = e^α + k×e^β + e^{-β}.
The architecture search selection has been implemented: the adapter from c^l with the highest weight λ is selected as the final A_n^l for each layer, completing the adapter assignment for task T_n.
The ordering constraint α > β > 0 has been implemented so that λ_e^l ≥ λ_j^l > λ_n^l, biasing selection toward empty or reused adapters rather than a new adapter.
The sparse factor α=0.11 and reuse factor β=0.08 have been set as hyperparameters for the architecture search weight initialization.

The pseudo-replay mechanism has been implemented: a language model generates pseudo-samples P_i from each reused prior task T_i, which are combined with current task data D_n to update the shared adapter in the tuning stage.
The layer-wise loss per layer has been implemented as Equation (3): L_n^l = 0 if A_n^l = A_e, otherwise L_n^l = cos(h_n^l, h_n^{l-1}).
The total layer-wise loss has been implemented as Equation (4): L_n = Σ_{l=1}^{L} L_n^l, summing the per-layer cosine similarity losses across all L transformer layers.
The layer-wise loss weight has been implemented as 0.4 for the similar scenario and 0.1 for the dissimilar scenario when adding L_n to the total training loss.
The no-AS constraint has been implemented: for the similar scenario, layer 5 is excluded from architecture search (always uses a new adapter); for the dissimilar scenario, layer 6 is excluded.
The training schedule has been implemented with 6 epochs for the decision stage (3 warmup epochs followed by 3 architecture search epochs) and 12 epochs for the tuning stage.
The pseudo-replay rate has been implemented as 0.2, generating pseudo-samples equal to 20% of the current task's training data.
The GPT-2 backbone (124M parameters) has been loaded with L=12 transformer layers and d=768 embedding/hidden dimensionality.
The AdamW optimizer has been implemented with a learning rate of 1.75e-4 and batch size of 8 for both the decision and tuning stages.
The similar scenario datasets (E2ENLG, RNNLG with restaurant/hotel/TV/laptop domains, SGD, TM19, TM20) have been loaded and preprocessed for all 14 domains with train/valid/test splits per Table 7.
The dissimilar scenario datasets (E2ENLG, RNNLG, WikiSQL, CNN/DailyMail, SGD, TM19, TM20) have been loaded and preprocessed for all 14 domains with train/valid/test splits per Table 7.
All eight task orders (Orders 1-4 for similar, Orders 5-8 for dissimilar) have been implemented as specified in Table 8 in Appendix C.
The INTENT task evaluation has been implemented using Accuracy (ACC) to compare predicted intent against the ground truth intent.
The DST task evaluation has been implemented using Joint Goal Accuracy (JGA), requiring exact match of both intent keyword and its value against the gold truth.
The NLG and Summarization task evaluation has been implemented using the BLEU score to measure similarity between generated text and reference text.
The SQL Query Generation task evaluation has been implemented using Exact Match (EM), requiring the generated SQL query to exactly match the gold SQL query.
The Average Score metric has been implemented as Score = (1/t) Σ_{i=1}^{t} R_{N,i}, averaging the test results on all tasks after all tasks have been learned.
The Backward Transfer (BWT) metric has been implemented as BWT = (1/(t-1)) Σ_{i=1}^{t-1} (R_{N,i} - R_{i,i}), measuring the effect of learning new tasks on previous task performance.
All 10 baselines have been implemented: Finetune, EWC, LAMOL, InsCL, AdapterCL, Adapter+LAMOL, ACM, O-LoRA, SAPT, and Multi (upper bound).
The ACM baseline has been implemented correctly including its adaptive module composition mechanism that decides whether to reuse or add a new adapter for each task.
The SAFM (w/o layer-wise) ablation variant has been implemented: SAFM with architecture search procedure but without the layer-wise loss during the tuning stage.
The pseudo-sample ratio ablation has been implemented with ratios {0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 0.8} for SAFM tested on Order 1 (similar) and Order 5 (dissimilar).
The no-AS layer index ablation has been implemented by fixing no AS at each of layers {3, 5, 7, 9} for Order 1 and {2, 4, 6, 8} for Order 5, with a 'Null' baseline using no fixed no-AS layer.
The scale-up experiments have been implemented with GPT-2-medium (355M) and GPT-2-large (774M) as backbone models, comparing SAFM and ACM on Order 1.
The Llama3-8B scale-up experiment has been implemented for comparing SAFM and ACM on the task sequence in Order 1, reporting scores and learnable parameters at task 8 and task 10.
The learnable parameter count has been tracked and reported for each method, measuring total trainable parameters across all adapter modules accumulated over the task sequence.
The main SAFM experiments on the similar scenario (Orders 1-4) have been executed successfully without errors, producing Score, BWT, and learnable parameter counts.
The main SAFM experiments on the dissimilar scenario (Orders 5-8) have been executed successfully without errors, producing Score, BWT, and learnable parameter counts.
All 10 baseline methods have been run successfully on both the similar and dissimilar scenarios, completing the full comparison in Table 1.

The ablation study experiments (ACM, SAFM w/o layer-wise, and full SAFM) have been executed successfully across all four task orders for both scenarios, reporting Score, BWT, and learnable parameters per order.
The pseudo-sample ratio ablation experiments with ratios {0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 0.8} have been executed successfully on Order 1 and Order 5.
The no-AS layer index ablation experiments have been executed successfully for ACM and SAFM across all selected layer indices in Order 1 (similar) and Order 5 (dissimilar).
The scale-up experiments with GPT-2 (124M, 355M, 774M) and Llama3-8B have been executed successfully for both SAFM and ACM in Order 1, reporting scores and parameter counts at task 8 and task 10.
The execution outcomes match Table 1: SAFM achieves Score 44.55% (±0.30) and BWT 0.60% (±0.84) with 14.88M learnable parameters in the similar scenario.
The execution outcomes match Table 1: SAFM achieves Score 51.38% (±0.12) and BWT 0.57% (±0.14) with 15.21M learnable parameters in the dissimilar scenario.
The execution outcomes match Table 1: SAFM is the only method to achieve positive BWT in both scenarios (0.60 and 0.57), with all baselines including AdapterCL showing non-positive BWT.

The execution outcomes match Table 1: SAFM achieves superior or comparable Score compared to AdapterCL (44.03 similar, 50.82 dissimilar) while using approximately 59% fewer learnable parameters (14.88M vs 25.06M).
The execution outcomes match Table 2: SAFM (w/o layer-wise) outperforms ACM across all task orders in both scenarios while using only 57.7%-70.2% of ACM's learnable parameters.

The execution outcomes match Table 2: Full SAFM (with layer-wise loss) outperforms SAFM (w/o layer-wise) across all task orders while using fewer parameters, confirming the positive impact of layer-wise loss.
The execution outcomes match Table 3: SAFM with only 0.1 pseudo-sample ratio achieves Score 42.71 (Order 1) and 51.43 (Order 5), outperforming ACM with 0.2 ratio (41.51 and 48.90).
The execution outcomes match Table 3: SAFM performance consistently improves as pseudo-sample ratio increases from 0.1 to 0.8 in both Order 1 and Order 5.

The execution outcomes match Table 4: Both ACM and SAFM show improved performance as the no-AS layer index increases in both scenarios, with the Null setting (no fixed no-AS) as the performance lower bound.
The execution outcomes match Figure 2: SAFM's learning curve in Order 5 is consistently above all compared methods across all tasks, confirming its superior continual learning performance.

The execution outcomes match Table 6: SAFM consistently outperforms ACM on all GPT-2 model sizes (124M, 355M, 774M) at both task 8 and task 10 in Order 1, with SAFM using significantly fewer learnable parameters.

The execution outcomes match Table 6: For Llama3-8B at task 8 in Order 1, SAFM achieves Score 42.78 with 1031.1M parameters versus ACM's Score 20.95 with 5279.3M parameters, demonstrating SAFM's superiority on large models.
